confirmed.title: findAll tasks allows limit=1000 â€” 10x higher than documented default, no hard cap at reasonable size
confirmed.code_evidence:
const safeLimit = Math.min(limit || 1000, 1000);
    const skip = (page - 1) * safeLimit;
confirmed.description: The default limit is `10` in the function signature (line 296) but `Math.min(limit || 1000, 1000)` means when limit is not passed the fallback is `10 || 1000 = 1000` because JS truthy: `10` is truthy so `10 || 1000 = 10`. However when a caller passes `limit=0` or `limit=null`, the fallback resolves to 1000 items, and callers can pass any value up to 1000. With the included joins (project, assignee, assignees.user, _count), 1000 tasks with their joins is a very heavy payload â€” easily 100KB+. The default parameter `limit = 10` is overridden immediately by the `|| 1000` on line 305.
confirmed.root_cause: The safeLimit expression `limit || 1000` substitutes 1000 for falsy values but also silently allows any caller to request up to 1000 rows with heavy includes.
confirmed.impact: A caller requesting limit=1000 gets up to 1000 task rows, each including nested project, assignee, multiple assignees with user details, and _count aggregates â€” the response can easily exceed 1 MB. Under concurrent load this is a DoS amplification vector.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Reduce the hard cap: `const safeLimit = Math.min(limit ?? 10, 100);`. If 1000 is a business requirement, add `select` projection to limit the columns returned and document the cap explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks?limit=1000 returns at most 100 rows (or the defined max)
GET /tasks with no limit returns the default page size (e.g. 10 or 20)
confirmed.notes: The same pattern appears in comments.service.ts and documents.service.ts (both use `Math.min(limit || 1000, 1000)` with default 1000). Adversarial note: the description's JS truthy reasoning is partially inverted (10 IS truthy, so default=10 path is correct; the problem is callers CAN pass limit=1000 explicitly). The substance of the finding is confirmed â€” the hard cap is 1000 with heavy includes.

confirmed.title: getTasksByAssignee and getTasksByProject have no pagination â€” unbounded findMany
confirmed.description: Both `getTasksByAssignee` (line 1133) and `getTasksByProject` (line 1240) call `findMany` with no `take` parameter. For a user assigned to hundreds of tasks, or a large project, all matching rows are returned in a single DB query plus all their includes (project, assignee, assignees+users, timeEntries, dependencies, subtasks). There is no pagination wrapper, no total count, and no hard cap.
confirmed.root_cause: Missing `take` and `skip` arguments on both unbounded `findMany` calls; no pagination DTO accepted by these methods.
confirmed.impact: A project with 500+ tasks will transfer all rows plus nested relations in one response. `getTasksByProject` additionally includes `subtasks` (unbounded per task), `dependencies`, and `assignees`. For large projects this can exceed several MB per request and cause OOM/timeout on the API process.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `take: 200` (or accept `page/limit` params) and return paginated meta. For `getTasksByProject` add a hard cap of e.g. 500 with a cursor or page param. Example: `take: Math.min(limit ?? 100, 500), skip: (page - 1) * take`.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks/assignee/:userId returns at most N rows (configurable, default â‰¤100)
GET /tasks/project/:projectId returns at most N rows and exposes meta.total

confirmed.title: getMyDoneUndeclaredTasks: unbounded findMany with NOT EXISTS subquery â€” full table scan risk
confirmed.description: No `take` limit on this query. The `NOT: { timeEntries: { some: { userId } } }` clause forces a NOT EXISTS correlated subquery against `time_entries` for every matched task. With tasks indexed on `assigneeId` and `status`, the outer scan is bounded, but the correlated NOT EXISTS runs a subquery per matched task row. No pagination is applied â€” all matching DONE tasks are returned at once.
confirmed.root_cause: Missing `take` limit and the NOT EXISTS correlated subquery may cause per-row subquery evaluation in older PostgreSQL planner versions.
confirmed.impact: A user who has completed many tasks and declared none of them (e.g. after a bulk import) gets all DONE tasks without time entries back in one shot. Performance degrades as the task set grows.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `take: 50` (or similar cap). For the NOT EXISTS pattern consider an alternative: LEFT JOIN time_entries on userId and filter WHERE te.id IS NULL, or use `findMany` with cursor pagination.
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks/my/done-undeclared returns at most 50 items per request

confirmed.title: findOrphans: unbounded findMany on tasks with projectId IS NULL â€” no pagination
confirmed.description: The `findOrphans` method returns all orphan tasks (projectId IS NULL) with no take/limit. The orphan set is unbounded; a government instance with many unattached tasks (meetings, cross-cutting work) could return thousands of rows with includes.
confirmed.impact: All orphan tasks returned in a single response with nested includes (assignee, assignees+users, _count). The API response grows linearly with the number of orphan tasks.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Accept `page` and `limit` query params and apply `take/skip`, or add a hard cap (e.g. `take: 200`).
acceptance_criteria: GET /tasks/orphans returns at most 200 rows or is paginated
confirmed.notes: CLAUDE.md explicitly notes 'Tasks without a project = intentional (meetings, cross-cutting work)' â€” so the orphan set is a first-class concern and can grow large.

confirmed.title: importTasks: sequential await inside for-loop â€” N DB round trips for task creation + M subtask inserts
confirmed.description: Inside the `for (let i = 0; i < tasks.length; i++)` loop: (1) one `findFirst` per task to check for duplicate title (N queries), (2) one `task.create` per task (N queries), (3) for each task with subtasks, one `subtask.create` per subtask (M queries total). There is no DTO-level array size limit for the import payload. With 100 tasks each having 5 subtasks, this is 100 + 100 + 500 = 700 sequential DB round trips within one HTTP request.
confirmed.root_cause: No batching: each loop iteration awaits individual Prisma calls instead of collecting and using `createMany` / batch transactions.
confirmed.impact: A 100-task import payload generates 200â€“700 sequential DB round trips. At 5ms per round trip this is 1â€“3.5 seconds of pure DB I/O on the request path. Larger imports (there is no size cap in the DTO) will timeout. Also no `take` limit on how many tasks the request body can contain.
confirmed.suggested_fix: 1. Pre-fetch all existing titles for the project in one query before the loop: `findMany({ where: { projectId }, select: { title: true } })`. 2. Use `prisma.task.createMany` for bulk insert (note: no nested relations, so subtasks need a separate `createMany`). 3. Add `@Max()` validation on `ImportTasksDto.tasks` array (e.g. max 500 items). 4. Wrap in a single transaction.
acceptance_criteria: POST /tasks/project/:id/import with 100 tasks completes in < 500ms
Import payload of > 500 tasks is rejected with 400
Duplicate detection uses a pre-fetched Set, not per-row findFirst
confirmed.notes: validateImport correctly pre-fetches existingTasks in one query (line 1558) â€” importTasks should mirror that pattern. Confirmed: ImportTasksDto has no @ArrayMaxSize. The 1 MiB bodyLimit (SEC-025) provides some protection but does not eliminate the N+1 pattern.

confirmed.title: checkCircularDependency: one DB query per BFS node â€” potential N+1 for deep dependency graphs
confirmed.description: The BFS loop issues one `taskDependency.findMany` per visited node. For a task graph with depth D and branching factor B, this is O(B^D) sequential DB queries in the worst case. There is no depth limit or node count limit. A malicious or accidentally deep dependency chain triggers one DB query per level.
confirmed.root_cause: BFS graph traversal with one query per visited node instead of a single recursive CTE or a bulk pre-fetch of the full dependency sub-graph.
confirmed.impact: Each `addDependency` call triggers this BFS. A project with 50+ tasks forming a chain causes 50 sequential DB queries on the write path. No timeout guard is present.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Option A (preferred): Use a PostgreSQL recursive CTE via `prisma.$queryRaw` to fetch the full reachable set in one query. Option B: Pre-fetch all `taskDependency` rows for the project in one query and run BFS entirely in memory. Add a max-depth guard of e.g. 20 hops before starting the BFS.
acceptance_criteria: addDependency on a chain of 50 tasks issues at most 1 DB query for cycle detection
Cycle detection is capped at a defined max depth (e.g. 20 hops)

confirmed.title: createBulkAssignment: one DB INSERT per (user Ă— date) pair â€” sequential awaits in nested loop, no transaction
confirmed.description: For N users and M dates, the nested loop issues NĂ—M individual `predefinedTaskAssignment.create` calls with sequential `await`. No transaction, no `createMany`. BulkAssignmentDto has `ArrayMinSize(1)` but no `ArrayMaxSize`, so a caller can pass 100 users Ă— 100 dates = 10,000 sequential DB inserts per HTTP request.
confirmed.root_cause: Missing `createMany` batching and missing array size caps on the DTO.
confirmed.impact: A request with 50 users Ă— 30 dates = 1,500 sequential DB round trips. At 5ms each = 7.5 seconds on the request path. The lack of a wrapping transaction means partial failures leave the data in an inconsistent state (some assignments created, others not).
confirmed.suggested_fix: 1. Add `@ArrayMaxSize(50)` on `userIds` and `@ArrayMaxSize(90)` on `dates` in BulkAssignmentDto. 2. Replace the nested loop with `prisma.predefinedTaskAssignment.createMany({ data: allPairs, skipDuplicates: true })` â€” wraps the entire batch in one statement. 3. Wrap in a transaction for atomicity.
acceptance_criteria: POST /predefined-tasks/assignments/bulk with 50 users Ă— 30 dates issues 1 DB statement instead of 1500
BulkAssignmentDto rejects userIds > 50 with 400
BulkAssignmentDto rejects dates > 90 with 400
confirmed.notes: Confirmed: BulkAssignmentDto has only @ArrayMinSize(1) on both userIds and dates â€” no upper bound. The 1 MiB bodyLimit limits the raw payload size but does not bound the number of DB round trips for a payload under 1 MiB.

confirmed.title: generateFromRules: one DB INSERT per occurrence date, sequential await inside nested for-loop
confirmed.description: Two nested loops: outer loop over all active rules, inner loop over all generated dates per rule. Each inner iteration `await`s a single `create`. For a 3-month range with 50 active rules, each weekly, this generates approximately 50 Ă— 13 = 650 sequential INSERT statements. There is no date range validation to prevent extremely wide ranges (e.g. 5 years), and no batch insert.
confirmed.root_cause: Missing `createMany` for the batch of assignments generated by a rule; missing date range cap in `GenerateFromRulesDto`.
confirmed.impact: A 6-month generation request with 100 weekly rules generates ~2,600 sequential DB inserts. At 5ms each = 13 seconds. This is a synchronous HTTP request path (no background job), so the connection times out.
confirmed.suggested_fix: 1. Add max date range validation in `GenerateFromRulesDto` (e.g. max 3 months). 2. Collect all dates across all rules and call `prisma.predefinedTaskAssignment.createMany({ data: allAssignments, skipDuplicates: true })` once. This collapses all inserts to 1 statement.
acceptance_criteria: POST /predefined-tasks/recurring-rules/generate issues at most 2 DB statements (1 select rules + 1 createMany assignments)
Date range > 3 months is rejected with 400
All assignments are atomic (succeed or fail together)
confirmed.notes: Confirmed: GenerateFromRulesDto has only @IsDateString()/@IsNotEmpty() on startDate and endDate â€” no max-range constraint. Any date range is accepted.

confirmed.title: bulkCreateRecurringRules: one DB INSERT per (user Ă— dayOfWeek) inside a transaction â€” sequential awaits
confirmed.description: Inside a transaction, two nested for-loops call `tx.predefinedTaskRecurringRule.create` sequentially per (user, dayOfWeek) pair. With N users and D days (1â€“7), this is NĂ—D sequential INSERTs inside a long-held transaction. `CreateBulkRecurringRulesDto` validates `userIds.length >= 1` and `daysOfWeek.length >= 1` but no max size cap.
confirmed.root_cause: Missing `createMany` batching; `create` in a loop inside a transaction holds the transaction open for NĂ—D round trips.
confirmed.impact: 50 users Ă— 7 days = 350 sequential DB round trips inside one transaction. Long-running transactions increase lock contention and can block other writers on the `predefined_task_recurring_rules` table.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Use `tx.predefinedTaskRecurringRule.createMany({ data: allRules })` after building the data array in memory. Add `@ArrayMaxSize` on both `userIds` and `daysOfWeek`.
acceptance_criteria: POST /predefined-tasks/recurring-rules/bulk issues 1 createMany statement instead of NĂ—D creates
DTO rejects userIds > 50 with 400
confirmed.notes: Confirmed: CreateBulkRecurringRulesDto has no @ArrayMaxSize on userIds. daysOfWeek is bounded by @Min(0)/@Max(6) per element (0-6 = at most 7 values) which partially mitigates the day dimension, but userIds has no upper bound.

confirmed.title: reorderSubtasks: one DB UPDATE per subtask in a transaction â€” N sequential round trips
confirmed.description: The reorder operation maps each subtask ID to a separate `update` call inside a Prisma interactive transaction. Prisma's array-form `$transaction` sends each update as a separate statement. For N subtasks this is N DB round trips inside one transaction. The `ReorderSubtasksDto` has no max array size.
confirmed.root_cause: No batched upsert or raw UPDATE â€¦ CASE statement; each position change is a separate DB query.
confirmed.impact: A task with 50 subtasks triggers 50 UPDATE statements per reorder request. While individually fast, this holds the transaction open and prevents concurrent subtask mutations. Under rapid UI drag-and-drop this can cause lock contention.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Use a raw SQL UPDATE â€¦ CASE â€¦ WHEN or `prisma.$executeRaw` to update all positions in a single statement. Add `@ArrayMaxSize(100)` to `ReorderSubtasksDto.subtaskIds`.
acceptance_criteria: POST /tasks/:id/subtasks/reorder with 50 subtasks issues 1 SQL statement
DTO validates subtaskIds.length <= 100
confirmed.notes: The impact is lower than P2-7/8 because subtask counts are typically small (< 20). Still a design smell. Confirmed: ReorderSubtasksDto has only @IsArray() and @IsUUID â€” no @ArrayMaxSize.

confirmed.title: TaskDependency.dependsOnTaskId has no index â€” reverse dependency lookups in checkCircularDependency are slow
confirmed.code_evidence:
model TaskDependency {
  id              String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId          String
  dependsOnTaskId String
  createdAt       DateTime @default(now())

  // Relations
  task          Task @relation("TaskDependencies", fields: [taskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  dependsOnTask Task @relation("DependentTasks", fields: [dependsOnTaskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId])
  @@map("task_dependencies")
}
confirmed.description: The `TaskDependency` model has a composite unique on `(taskId, dependsOnTaskId)` which gives a forward index on `taskId` (leading column). However there is no index on `dependsOnTaskId` alone. The `tasks.service.ts findOne` includes `dependents: { include: { task: ... } }` which executes a lookup by `dependsOnTaskId`. Without an index, this is a seq-scan on `task_dependencies` filtered by `dependsOnTaskId`.
confirmed.root_cause: The reverse FK column `dependsOnTaskId` is not covered by any index (the unique only has `taskId` as leading column).
confirmed.impact: Every `findOne(task)` executes a full table scan on `task_dependencies` to find all tasks that depend on the fetched task. As the dependency graph grows, this scales linearly.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add to schema.prisma: `@@index([dependsOnTaskId]) // reverse dependency lookup`
acceptance_criteria: EXPLAIN on `SELECT * FROM task_dependencies WHERE depends_on_task_id = $1` shows Index Scan, not Seq Scan
Migration is generated: `CREATE INDEX task_dependencies_depends_on_task_id_idx ON task_dependencies (depends_on_task_id)`
confirmed.verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U postgres -d orchestra -c "EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM task_dependencies WHERE depends_on_task_id = 'test';"

confirmed.title: TaskRACI has no index on userId â€” per-user RACI lookups require seq-scan
confirmed.code_evidence:
model TaskRACI {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId    String
  userId    String
  role      RACIRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  // Relations
  task Task @relation(fields: [taskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([taskId, userId, role])
  @@map("task_raci")
}
confirmed.description: The `TaskRACI` model has only a composite unique on `(taskId, userId, role)`. The `taskId`-leading unique covers per-task lookups. However, there is no index on `userId` alone. Any query filtering RACI entries by `userId` (e.g. 'all tasks where user X is Accountable') requires a full table scan. Although the current codebase doesn't expose such a direct query, the `findOne` task detail page does include `raci: { select: { id, userId, role, createdAt } }` without a userId filter.
confirmed.root_cause: Missing `@@index([userId])` on `TaskRACI` â€” the reverse FK on `userId` is not covered by any index (the unique has `taskId` as leading column).
confirmed.impact: If a future query fetches 'all RACI entries for user X' or if the table grows large, per-userId filtering will seq-scan. Note: the cascade delete concern mentioned in the original notes is partially incorrect â€” `userId` in TaskRACI has NO Prisma FK relation to User (only the `task` relation exists); PostgreSQL therefore has no FK index requirement from User â†’ TaskRACI, and no cascade delete path from User. The index concern for direct query performance remains valid.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([userId]) // DAT-011: FK without auto-index` to the TaskRACI model.
acceptance_criteria: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM task_raci WHERE user_id = $1 shows Index Scan
Migration generates: CREATE INDEX task_raci_user_id_idx ON task_raci (user_id)
confirmed.verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U postgres -d orchestra -c "EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM task_raci WHERE user_id = 'test';"
confirmed.notes: DOWNGRADED from medium to low. The cascade-delete rationale is incorrect: TaskRACI.userId has no Prisma FK relation to User (only to Task). No cascade trigger from User deletion will hit task_raci via a DB FK. The index would only help if a userId-only query is ever issued, which the current service layer does not do. Real impact is limited to hypothetical future queries.

confirmed.title: PredefinedTaskAssignment has no index on predefinedTaskId â€” per-task assignment lookups may seq-scan
confirmed.code_evidence:
model PredefinedTaskAssignment {
  id                  String    @id @default(uuid())
  predefinedTaskId    String
  userId              String
  date                DateTime
  period              DayPeriod // DAT-012: was String "MORNING"|"AFTERNOON"|"FULL_DAY"
  assignedById        String?
  isRecurring         Boolean   @default(false)
  recurringRuleId     String?   
  completionStatus    AssignmentCompletionStatus @default(NOT_DONE) // DAT-012: was String
  completedAt         DateTime?
  completedById       String?   
  notApplicableReason String?
  createdAt           DateTime  @default(now())
  updatedAt           DateTime  @updatedAt

  @@unique([predefinedTaskId, userId, date, period])
  @@index([date, userId])
  @@index([completionStatus, date])
confirmed.description: The unique constraint on `(predefinedTaskId, userId, date, period)` has `predefinedTaskId` as the leading column, so per-task queries (`WHERE predefinedTaskId = X`) use this unique index. However, queries on `(userId)` alone (which is the second column of the unique) cannot use the unique index efficiently. The `findAssignments` method in the service filters by `userId` and optionally `predefinedTaskId` and `date`; when only `userId` is supplied, the `[date, userId]` composite index can be used (userId is the trailing column), but filtering by `userId` only (no date) would require a full scan.
confirmed.root_cause: No dedicated `@@index([userId])` for the `predefinedTaskId` + `userId` filter combination when predefinedTaskId is not present.
confirmed.impact: When `findAssignments({ userId })` is called without `startDate`/`endDate`, the query cannot use the `[date, userId]` index efficiently because `date` is the leading column. This is a seq-scan risk as assignments grow.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `@@index([userId]) // reverse-lookup for per-user assignment queries` to `PredefinedTaskAssignment`.
acceptance_criteria: EXPLAIN on SELECT ... FROM predefined_task_assignments WHERE user_id = $1 shows Index Scan
confirmed.verification_command: docker exec orchestr-a-db psql -U postgres -d orchestra -c "EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM predefined_task_assignments WHERE user_id = 'test';"
confirmed.notes: The `[date, userId]` composite index covers the common (date-scoped) query path. This finding only applies to unbounded userId-only queries.

confirmed.title: findAssignments (predefined-tasks): no pagination and no maximum result cap
confirmed.description: The `findAssignments` method has no `take` limit. A request with `?startDate=2020-01-01&endDate=2030-01-01` returns all assignments in a 10-year window, potentially thousands of rows with included relations.
confirmed.impact: Wide date-range queries return unbounded result sets with includes (predefinedTask with multiple fields, user, assignedBy). Memory and response-size risk for large data sets.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a reasonable default cap: `take: Math.min(filters.limit ?? 500, 1000)`. Alternatively add pagination params to the query DTO.
acceptance_criteria: GET /predefined-tasks/assignments with wide date range returns at most 1000 rows

confirmed.title: findRecurringRules: no pagination â€” unbounded findMany with includes
confirmed.description: The method returns all active recurring rules matching the optional filters, with no `take` or pagination. Includes nested predefinedTask, user, and createdBy.
confirmed.impact: If many users have many recurring rules (e.g. 100 users Ă— 5 rules each = 500 rows) and no filter is applied, the full set is returned in one response with nested includes.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `take: 500` as a hard cap or accept pagination params.
acceptance_criteria: GET /predefined-tasks/recurring-rules returns at most 500 rows

confirmed.title: findAll comments uses default limit=1000 â€” very high default page size
confirmed.description: The default `limit` is 1000 in the function signature, and `safeLimit` is capped at 1000. A caller who hits `GET /comments` without pagination parameters receives up to 1000 comment rows. Each row includes `author` and `task` (with `select: { id, title }`). While comment rows are lighter than task rows, 1000 comments in one response is still over-fetching.
confirmed.root_cause: Overly high default limit of 1000 â€” should be 20â€“50.
confirmed.impact: Default response includes up to 1000 comments. For busy tasks this degrades performance and wastes bandwidth.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change to `limit = 20` and cap at 100: `const safeLimit = Math.min(limit ?? 20, 100);`
acceptance_criteria: GET /comments without limit param returns at most 20 items
GET /comments?limit=200 is capped at 100

confirmed.title: findAll documents uses default limit=1000 â€” very high default page size
confirmed.description: Same pattern as comments: default limit of 1000, hard cap at 1000. Each document row includes `project: { id, name }`. A project with hundreds of documents returns them all in one response without pagination.
confirmed.impact: GET /documents without pagination returns up to 1000 document metadata rows.
acceptance_criteria: GET /documents without limit param returns at most 20 items
